clinical_coherence.notes: 67M African American with HTN, DM2, hyperlipidemia, OSA, CKD3a, prior TIA. Wake-up stroke with right hemiparesis and subtle right-sided neglect. Vitals show hypertension. No known AF -- the key moderate element is that AF is occult and discovered on prolonged monitoring. The clinical presentation with subtle neglect and dysarthria is well-crafted for a moderate case.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Excellent concordance. MRI shows DWI+/FLAIR+ in left MCA superior division (indicating onset >4.5h, excluding tPA). ECG shows sinus rhythm with LAE -- the clue to occult AF. CTA shows moderate left ICA stenosis with vulnerable plaque. Holter reveals paroxysmal AF. All findings build a coherent mixed-mechanism story.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD I63.9 used but appropriate given mixed etiology. Diagnosis correctly identifies mixed mechanism (large artery atherosclerosis + newly discovered paroxysmal AF). Critical actions excellently include DWI-FLAIR mismatch evaluation, recognition of LAE as AF clue, prolonged cardiac monitoring, and transition from antiplatelet to anticoagulation. Contraindicated actions well-calibrated.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Perfectly calibrated as moderate. Wake-up stroke requiring tissue-clock evaluation (DWI-FLAIR mismatch), initially cryptogenic-appearing with normal sinus rhythm on ECG, requiring synthesis of LAE clue + prolonged monitoring to discover paroxysmal AF. The mixed mechanism adds appropriate complexity.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 67M African American with metabolic syndrome cluster is highly realistic. Multiple poorly controlled risk factors. Prior TIA adds history. PMH still follows the amlodipine/losartan/metformin/glipizide template seen in straightforward cases, but with more individualized details (OSA, prior TIA).

educational_value.notes: Outstanding case teaching wake-up stroke management, DWI-FLAIR mismatch concept, significance of LAE on ECG, importance of prolonged cardiac monitoring, and the pivotal antiplatelet-to-anticoagulation transition. Multiple clinical pearls. Would discriminate well between good and poor diagnostic agents.

strengths: Excellent difficulty calibration with genuine diagnostic uncertainty
LAE as a subtle clue to occult AF is clinically authentic
